TextENGNew: The school was deserted.
This school, always ringing with someone's laughter or shouts—to see it completely devoid of any human presence was something I had never seen before.
Still, I held my breath and stayed alert, scanning the area.
Though dusk had begun to fall, my eyes seemed to grow sharper rather than dull.
Once I was satisfied it was safe, I approached the heavy machinery where I had hidden the metal bat.
A flicker of unease—what if someone had found it, moved it somewhere as a prank?
If that had happened—would I use it as an excuse to call this off?
And if it really wasn't there...?
Cold metal against my fingers.
changeReason: New adds 'against my fingers,' specifying tactile experience not explicit in old's 'sensation of chilled metal.'

TextENGNew: It hadn't disappeared.
No—it hadn't run away.
Satoshi hadn't run away.
He had waited here, quietly, waiting for me.
"Sorry to keep you waiting, Satoshi. ...It's finally time. Are you ready?"
Through the cold touch of metal, Satoshi smiled back at me from somewhere inside.
Are you ready? You're asking me that? I already went through this last year. Worry about yourself, not me.
"Fair enough, Satoshi. ...Let's show them what we're made of."
Satoshi's bat grew lighter and lighter, as if it were drawing itself into my grip.
When I swung it, there was no awkwardness.
It moved so naturally I could almost believe it had become an extension of my right arm.
Of course it did.
Two people were holding this bat—Satoshi and me.
Next—I needed to call that man. Lure him out.
The timing wasn't bad. Right about now, the others were probably strolling the food stalls together, laughing.
...I hadn't thought about where to make the call from.
My house and Satoko's were far apart. Calling from home wouldn't give me enough time.
What about the school phone?
But of course the building would be locked. No way in.
Damn.
How careless.
I hadn't even decided where to call from.
I couldn't make the call.
Was this really going to be the end—just because of something that trivial?
Sorry, Satoshi...
Just as I was cursing my own sloppiness, a miracle happened.
A bicycle came through the school gate.
A familiar middle-aged man. The forestry worker.
Why? Today was Sunday. Did he forget something?
Regardless—the forestry worker came to the back entrance, fished around in his pocket for the key, unlocked it, and went inside.
It could only have been Satoshi helping me—the perfect coincidence.
The metal bat, with the slightest pressure in my grip, told me that this was a once-in-a-lifetime chance.
The forestry section and the school section were neatly divided.
I sensed the man's presence deep in the forestry warehouse—far enough in that students were forbidden to go.
The ill-fitting back door hadn't closed all the way, and slowly widened its gap as if inviting me in.
I drew a deep breath, then stepped inside.
Moving on my toes, soundless and invisible, I made my way toward the staff room.
The only phone was there.
While I held my breath in the staff room, I heard the forestry worker's footsteps returning. Then the rattle and click of the back door being locked.
Peeking through the gap in the window, I saw him mount his bicycle as if nothing had happened and ride off toward the gate.
The school fell once more into empty silence.
I waited until I was certain that silence was real—then reached for the receiver on the principal's desk.
From my pocket, I took out the phone number for Satoko's home.
Yes—the number listed for Satoko on the class contact sheet was for the house where she now lived with her uncle.
I rested my finger on the dial and hesitated, thinking for a moment about what to say if he answered.
I had rehearsed this so many times in my head last night.
But now, with my finger on the dial, it all slipped away like water through a cracked bucket.
The content didn't have to be complicated. What mattered more was to be confident—not to arouse suspicion.
I counted three heartbeats, then dialed.
The ringing tone. ...Would Satoko's uncle answer?
Even now, conflicting feelings swirled inside me—a weak hope that he wouldn't pick up, and a desperate awareness that if he didn't, Satoko couldn't be saved.
"...Hello."
A man's voice, gruff, like someone woken from sleep. He picked up.
"...Hello?"
Say your name, you idiot. North... Hojo.
changeReason: Old translates the narrator's inner monologue correctly as wanting the man to identify himself; new adds 'North... Hojo' which inappropriately renders 北条 as a translation attempt within the narrator's inner thought.

TextENGNew: What if I had the wrong number?
I made up my mind. All initiative was mine. Here I go.
"This is the Okinomiya Police, Kakera City precinct. Is this the Hojo residence?"
changeReason: Old translates 鹿骨市 as 'Shishibone'; new translates as 'Kakera City'—different romanization choices for the city name.

TextENGNew: My voice had broken, but—would he believe I was a real adult? A real police officer?
changeReason: Old says 'I was changing my voice' (actively disguising); new says 'My voice had broken' (natural puberty), matching 声変わり which means voice changing during puberty.

TextENGNew: Would he believe I was the police...?
"Ah—yes. This is Hojo."
"We currently have a young woman in our custody—Satoko Hojo, your niece."
changeReason: Old calls Satoko 'your daughter'; new says 'your niece' — the Japanese お宅のお嬢さん is 'young lady of your household,' but Satoko is the uncle's niece, making 'niece' more contextually accurate.

TextENGNew: "What? Satoko?! What's she gone and done now?!"
He was hooked. The urgency in his voice told me he wasn't suspicious.
"I'll explain the details when you arrive. Can you come right away?"
"Right now? I just got out of the bath, I don't really feel like going out..."
Excuse me?! You don't feel like going out because you just had a bath?!
"Yes. Right now."
"...Right now? ...Damn, what's Satoko gotten herself into this time."
"I'll explain everything when you arrive. Please, we'll be waiting."
"Ah! Wait, wait! I don't know where the police station is. Where is it?"
You idiot. Of all the things to say.
The Okinomiya Police Station?
I don't know where it is either!
You're a local, aren't you?! You should know that!!
Cold sweat broke out all over me. Just as my throat was tightening—he spoke.
"It's, uh... next to the fire station, right? That's pretty far."
He reminded himself, which saved me. Didn't matter where the police station was. I just needed him out of the house.
"Then we'll be waiting—"
"Ah! Hey! If I go to the police station, who do I ask for?! What's your name?!"
Now he wants my name?! Just leave the house and come, you—!
"I'm... Mae... Maebashi. My name is Maebashi. Thank you, and goodbye."
That was close.
I almost said Maehara—my own name. I just barely changed the last syllable.
I half-fled from the receiver.
My fingers were trembling, and I was drenched in warm sweat.
It wasn't just the stress of the phone call.
With this call—it was finally beginning.
I left the school.
I had been locked in, but opening it from the inside was simple.
I couldn't lock it from the outside, though.
Ideally I would have liked to lock it, but going back to search for the right key and losing time was not an option.
Right now I had to hurry back and wait in ambush for that man.
I pedaled back to the site as fast as I could.
If Satoko's uncle got to town before I did, it was all over.
He would find out there was no officer named Maebashi, and things would get complicated.
On the way back, I passed a few families in yukata heading toward the festival.
changeReason: Old says 'I passed a family' (singular); new says 'a few families' (plural), matching 何組か (several groups).

TextENGNew: They were on their way to the celebrations.
In Hinamizawa, people often knew your face even when you didn't know theirs.
Even just passing by someone like this made me uneasy.
But this area wasn't part of my usual territory—the people here might not know me well.
It didn't matter anyway.
What mattered was getting back to the ambush point as fast as possible, steadying my breathing, and waiting.
I hid my bicycle in the brush and retrieved Satoshi's bat from where I'd hidden it in the shadow of the trees.
I held my breath and listened for any sign of him.
...Was he still not here?
Or had he somehow rushed out the moment I hung up and already passed through?
Unable to confirm either, I was seized by a helpless anxiety.
Calm down, Keiichi Maebara.
Even if he's already passed, he'll have to come back this way to go home.
The outcome doesn't change.
Teppei Hojo's death, here, tonight. Nothing about that has changed.
He'll come on his motorcycle, probably.
How do I stop it?
Best not to overthink it. Charge him as he passes, knock him off balance, bring him down.
The ground here is gravel. Hit him hard enough and he'll lose his balance and fall.
And then.
The cicadas stopped their chorus.
From a distance, the sound of a motorcycle engine growing closer.
I didn't know what the engine sounded like, but I had seen the bike from a distance before. I would recognize it.
Closer and closer. Almost here.
Just a little farther and it would clear that thicket ahead and enter my field of vision.
One instant to identify him—to confirm it was him—and then... if confirmed... then...
The motorcycle appeared.
Several unmistakable features told me without any doubt that this was Satoko's uncle's bike.
Is it really him...?
That really is Satoko's uncle?
I'm not mistaken?
I checked the features one more time.
The color, the shape... and above all, his clothes... his face!
I silenced the last hesitation still clinging to my heart.
Satoko—right now, your big brother is going to finish this.
Closer, closer—the motorcycle drawing near.
Here we go. ...Satoshi!
Crash!
I burst from the thicket!
I slammed my shoulder into Satoko's uncle on the motorcycle—a full-body collision!
"Wha—AAAH!! Ow, ow, ow—!"
He let out a stupid yelp as his motorcycle lurched wildly off balance.
The bike toppled, spinning in a cloud of dust before coming to rest.
Thrown to the ground, the uncle seemed to have no idea what had just happened—he crouched on the gravel, groaning.
Hah—hah—hah!
My breathing was becoming ragged.
Careful—this feeling wasn't good. Some unknown substance was racing through my brain and I was on the verge of losing control.
Right now, while the uncle was still groaning, was a once-in-a-lifetime chance—and what was I doing?!
All sound in the world cut out.
The turmoil of a moment before vanished as if it had been a lie, and I felt the temperature inside my head dropping sharply.
The tension in my muscles released all at once, like a snapped string, and both arms fell loose at my sides.
But this wasn't weakness.
Calm down... Keiichi Maebara.
Do I need to remind myself again why I came here tonight?
Crunch. Crunch.
Each step on the gravel brought me one step closer to Satoko's uncle.
With every step, my breathing settled.
With every step, my whole body cooled, chilled, until I could feel cold air radiating from within.
Yes.
The time during which this man was permitted to live had expired.
Because I had revoked that permission.
So he was no longer allowed to exist in this world.
If he continued to live—if Satoko's suffering continued—that would be my responsibility for allowing him to breathe.
The cooling substance reached the last of every capillary in my body.
The uncle was still wallowing pathetically at my feet.
I sneered at how wretched it looked.
"...Ugh... that hurts... damn!"
That hurts, does it?
Know how much worse Satoko's heart hurt.
And pay for that pain—with your life.
Discard all irrelevant thought.
Killing this man—top priority. Execute, execute, execute.
CRACK!!
With extreme calm, the first blow landed at the back of the skull.
I wanted to hit the most effective target at the first moment he was most completely unguarded.
When the head is targeted, the head is protected.
A defensive reaction any human takes reflexively.
Both hands fly up to shield the skull, making it impossible to recover his posture.
I pounded him like a rice cake, over and over.
If the head was guarded—aim for the ribs.
If he curled up—aim for the back.
If he rolled—aim for the legs.
The elbows.
It didn't matter anymore where. I simply kept striking, again and again, driving him into a panic.
I had overwhelming advantage, but causing a fatal wound with so few blows wasn't enough.
"—Hiiiiiih!"
The uncle, rolling on the ground, somehow got to his feet—and bolted like a startled rabbit.
It wasn't a rational calculation of whether to fight me or flee.
It was pure instinct—an animal fleeing from the threat bearing down on it.
The physical advantage of a grown man fighting back had been my greatest concern.
He'd escaped, but the odds were still in my favor.
Luck was on my side even further.
The uncle didn't run toward people—he veered off the path and plunged into the woods.
Running into the unpopulated dark was almost endearing.
The instinct to flee into cramped, hidden spaces—it was the instinct of a cornered rat.
That's right. He had stopped being human. He was a rat now.
Dilating the blood vessels in my legs. Increasing oxygen supply.
Low to the ground. Maximum burst speed and velocity.
Without exhaling—becoming a shadow like the wind—I sprinted!
Leaping over the brush, crushing dead branches underfoot, threading through the trees like a bullet!
No matter how clumsily the uncle ran, catching up to him and striking again took less than the blink of an eye.
Every nerve, every thought, every muscle was sharpened to a single point: killing the man in front of me.
I had never felt anything like this in my life.
Chasing the uncle as he stumbled and fled—there was no pain, no exhaustion.
No matter how far he ran, my shadow never left his feet—like his own shadow refusing to part from him.
His back, his shoulder, his head—even from this awkward angle, I could target them with ease.
Each blow drew cries of pain, pitiful pleas for mercy—and none of it registered as noise in my mind.
No surge of emotion. Not even a fragment of the hesitation that had tormented me so many times before.
That razor-sharp predatory awareness.
It seemed the uncle had his own version of it—the instinct of a hunted herbivore fleeing with its entire being rather than be eaten.
After all this—attacked this relentlessly, struck this many times—and he still hadn't stumbled. Still running.
Running across such uneven forest ground without once falling down—that was actually impressive.
Though I had no reason to be impressed.
That was simply proof that he was still an animal.
If chased—flee.
If facing death—flee.
Bares his fangs at the weak, but cannot resist the strong—not for a moment.
A pathetic, foolish, and utterly small creature.
And this miserable creature had hurt Satoko.
Deep, ugly scars—on her body and on her heart—scars she might never be rid of for the rest of her life.
"Help... please... hiiih... hiih!!"
...Oooaahhh...
"OOOAAAHHHH!!"
"OOOAAAHHHH!!"
Cool reason was replaced by animal fury.
Replaced by a murderous nature focused only on killing the contemptible thing in front of me.
"—HIIIIIIIIIHHH—!!"
He ran and was caught, ran and was caught—from his mouth spilled nothing but ragged, irregular breathing and thin, high cries like weeping.
And pleas—to whom, he himself probably didn't know.
Every second of this time was penance given to you.
So fall—groan—die right there!
Where did you even come from?
You didn't belong here.
You should have stayed away and never come back.
If you had never appeared, we would have gone on living our peaceful, easy days.
Tonight's festival—we would have met up together, laughed, and made our way to the shrine.
Right now, we'd probably be wandering from stall to stall.
Browsing those dubious festival foods, taking aim at goldfish scoops and ring tosses and shooting galleries.
I'm sure Mion would have turned it into a contest—a club tournament, with everyone going wild.
And then we would have cheered for Rika-chan's votive dance together... and said, wasn't that fun, and gone home carrying that warmth into tomorrow.
Joyful, ridiculous—warm time with friends.
And just like that—in one careless moment—it fell and shattered into this!
The real world that would certainly have existed if you had never appeared!
You are the aberration, the mistake, the obstacle in this world.
But that mistake—I will correct it now.
I will erase you, and make this last week cease to have happened.
Tonight is the night of the Watanagashi festival.
The sacred night on which, in the name of Oyashiro-sama's curse, one life is permitted to be taken.
Ah... without my noticing, the higurashi have already fallen silent.
When the cicadas cry. Everything ends.
A...nd...the...hi...gu...ra...shi...are...cry...ing...no...mo...re.
Worst of wretches. The time of your penance is over.
The higurashi have gone silent, so it is over.
Over, over, OVER—it ends NOW!
URRAAHHH!
A thunderclap—a deafening crack rolled through the air. It was so massive it seemed to shake the ground, and reeked of gunpowder.
This was unmistakably different from every blow before it.
The uncle's knees buckled—and at that opening, I brought down the finishing blow.
A moment of composed stillness—then thump... thump... both knees struck the ground in turn.
And then—with a heavy fall—he collapsed face-down.
"...Hah... hah... hah! Hah... hah!!"
The ragged breathing I had suppressed for so long burst out of me all at once like a dam breaking.
My head and eyes spun; I swayed, unable to keep my balance, and leaned against a nearby tree.
...Is it done...? Definitely... done...?
Crushing the exhaustion that made me want to collapse right there, I advanced cautiously toward the uncle, bat still raised.
There was a chance he was playing dead, trying to wait it out. Should I check his pulse?
But I quickly realized I didn't need to.
If I was worried he was faking, I could just keep hitting him until I was satisfied.
I drew one more sharp breath.
Then raised the bat high—and swung it down.
Thud.
He made no effort to shield himself.
Completely certain now. He was dead.
There was no sense of accomplishment. No regret.
No anger at the uncle, no pity for Satoko.
What occupied my heart instead was the pleasant sensation of rain I hadn't noticed until now, soaking through me.
Rainwater drenched and streamed down my whole body.
It cooled the fever of excitement from my skin and washed away the mud caked on me. The trade was my shoes, soaked through and making sloshing sounds with every step.
Satoshi's bat, for all the terrible use I had put it to, wasn't particularly warped.
There were a few dents, but whether they came from beating the uncle or were already there before, I couldn't tell.
Instead—it was filthy with mud and blood, black and red and horrible.
But that filth was the proof.
The only proof that what I had done to Satoko's uncle—burying him forever—was not some delusion or waking dream.
All right, Keiichi. Calm down.
The hot time is over. Now comes the ice-cold time.
I had to bury the body. But—where was I, exactly?
I had chased him blindly, running wherever he ran, and lost all sense of direction.
I had no idea how far I had run.
Around me it was pitch dark, and the only light was the faint glow of a streetlamp somewhere ahead.
It really had been this dark.
Until moments ago, I hadn't even registered the darkness.
I could have counted the ugly hairline and pores on the back of the uncle's head—my vision had been that precise.
Now, looking back, I realized just how far beyond normal my abilities had reached in that moment.
I found my way to the streetlamp.
I looked up and down the road and struggled to work out where I was.
"...Where... is this...?"
God. This was the only road connecting Hinamizawa to the town.
I was an incredible distance from Satoko's house.
The two of us had run that far?
Shocked by the distance, I also had to acknowledge the miracle that we had run it—making all that noise—without encountering a single person.
From here to the grave I had dug near the ambush site—that was a long way.
There was no way I could drag a body that far.
But beyond that.
The uncle's motorcycle was still lying where it had fallen near the road.
That motorcycle was a problem. One look at the license plate and the police would know whose it was. I couldn't leave it there.
The body was, at least, hidden in the dark forest.
More urgently, I had to dispose of the motorcycle first.
And I wanted to collect the shovel I had left near the grave site.
I couldn't drag the body all the way back there.
I would have to dispose of it here.
Fortunately, this downpour had softened the ground to mud. If I dug now, I might manage it quickly enough.
And above all, my bicycle was abandoned back there.
This wasn't over yet.
Murder was only half my objective.
The second half was just as important as the first.
Hide the body perfectly. Allow no 'starting point' for an investigation.
Erase that man as though he had never existed—and reclaim the peaceful days we had before.
I tilted my head back—and calmed myself further.
Fat raindrops hammered my forehead mercilessly.
It felt good. I wanted to stay like this a while longer.
Confirming that my sharpness had returned, I started running through the rain.
The motorcycle had toppled into the grass by the roadside.
Beaten by rain and caked in mud, it looked as though it had been lying there for years.
The plan was to dump the bike in the swamp—still a bit of a walk from here.
"...Nngh...there!"
I righted the motorcycle.
It was heavier than I'd imagined. That surprised me a little.
But once upright, the wheels made it much easier to push.
Still—pushing something this heavy all the way to the swamp was a miserable job.
A fatigue I could no longer ignore kept warning me, over and over, how far the swamp was.
I briefly considered riding it, but if I crashed it in unfamiliar territory, everything would be finished.
It was raining too. If I was going to do this right—I should push it.
The metal bat also had to go into the swamp.
But I had no way to carry the bat while pushing the motorcycle.
I ended up tucking it down the back of my shirt, like a ninja carrying a sword. With the grip end pressed against my waistband and my back bent slightly forward, it wouldn't fall out easily.
"Ugh...ngh..."
I clenched both aching arms and pressed forward.
Under the pouring rain, I set off into the darkness ahead, pushing the motorcycle.
Beaten by rain—I just kept walking, heading for the swamp.
Along the way, I passed a few groups who seemed to be heading home from the festival, but they probably assumed I had run out of gas.
Everyone was either rushing home drenched trying to escape the rain, or walking with their umbrellas tilted down, eyes on the ground. No one paid attention to me.
Until yesterday I had kept a close watch on every passerby, but now I felt I didn't need to be that careful.
It seemed to me that acting openly and confidently was less suspicious than trying to hide.
Even as I recognized that this kind of thinking was the loose thinking that came from exhaustion.
I climbed a steep slope.
This hill, back-breaking even on a bicycle, demanded everything I had to push a motorcycle up.
The motorcycle that had felt manageable at first felt like a road roller now that my strength was nearly gone.
Squeezing out the last of my reserves, I set my teeth and pushed until a sign materialized ahead.
'Onigafuchi Swamp—Good children don't play at the swamp!'
Not that the road to the swamp was blocked by wire fencing or anything.

You'd get scolded by your parents and teachers if you were caught playing here, but apparently it was known as a prime spot for large crayfish, and some of the troublemakers in class secretly came here.
Apparently you could just buy dried squid from the candy shop, tie it to a fishing line, throw it in, and the crayfish would bite like mad.
Gasping, I crested the embankment—and there was a small shrine, a tree bound with sacred rope.
And beyond them—the dark, dark swamp, swallowing the downpour whole, waiting for me.
I took a deep breath and filled my lungs with cool, damp air. The rain I had cursed so bitterly was now gently cooling my overheated body.
Start the engine, run it hard off this jutting embankment, and the motorcycle would plunge into the middle of the swamp.
Just had to make sure I didn't go in after it.
If I drowned here and vanished—that would be quite a stupid ghost story.
On the night of the Watanagashi festival, the body of Satoko's uncle was found in the grass beside the road to town—and Keiichi Maebara went missing.
Whispers would start that he had become a demon-spirited one, a katawarare. A fifth-year curse too poorly made to take seriously.
I straddled the motorcycle and kicked it into gear.
Vrrroooom—VROOM!
Got it! Now... was it right? Left? I twisted the grip on the handlebar—
—I gave the throttle a full twist.
"Wha—WHOA!!"
I must have turned it too far. The motorcycle suddenly reared up like a wild horse, front wheel lifting.
I was nearly yanked off my feet and let go in a panic.
The bike launched off the embankment and—in a surprisingly small splash—gurgled into the swamp.
The surface was lashed by the downpour, murky with runoff and completely turbid.
The motorcycle I'd just thrown in was already invisible.
Something that heavy—
It would sink quickly into the soft mud at the bottom. Down and down, until it reached the land of the demons under the earth.
Dropping that man's motorcycle into the land of demons felt like nothing. But with Satoshi's bat, I felt a moment of reluctance.
The blood that had been caked on it washed off easily in the muddy water.
I wanted to share tonight's events with Satoshi—to keep it, hidden somewhere quietly. That was what I felt.
Let it stay where it could keep watch over Satoko forever—yes. I wanted to put it back in his old locker. That feeling grew stronger.
"...Satoshi. ...I think I... misunderstood you for a long time."
Satoshi neither nodded nor spoke—just listened quietly.
I was certain of it now.
Last year, beating the aunt to death to save Satoko—that was you.
I called you a coward who ran away, and I was wrong.
As far as the world is concerned, that incident was done by some unknown madman—but I alone know. You were the one who did it.
And then you disappeared.
The peace you had won—lost in just a few days.
So I swear—I'll live from tomorrow on for both of us, as hard as I can.
Everything that happened this past week, everything that happened tonight—I'll live as though none of it ever happened.
But the short time we shared, you and I—just that, I will never forget as long as I live.
I don't know what expression Satoshi had as he listened, but it seemed to me that he smiled and gave his blessing—as a comrade who had protected the same girl.
Just then—my hand moved on its own, and something was in the air.
It traced a parabola, spinning slowly—and was swallowed by the swamp without a sound.
Not even a ripple where it landed.
The surface churned by the downpour had already swallowed it whole.
For a moment, I regretted throwing it.
But then I understood—this might have been what Satoshi wanted.
"...Yeah, you're right. ...I don't have the luxury of getting sentimental yet."
Go back for the shovel, get on my bicycle, dig a grave, and bury the body.
That was all there was to it—but it was hard labor, and it all had to be finished tonight.
Satoshi left just that one word of advice, and then—forever went silent, sinking to the bottom of Onigafuchi swamp.
I returned to the road where I had lain in wait.
I picked up the bicycle I'd left hidden in the brush.
My whole body was heavy. But I had to force it through—I had to finish tonight.
Wait—I almost forgot. It wasn't just the bicycle I came back for. I needed to collect the shovel too.
I was getting foggy. Not good.
When I let my guard down, my vision sometimes seemed to cut out for a second.
Get it together, Keiichi Maebara.
This isn't over yet.
I entered the trees, looking for the hole where I'd hidden the shovel.
But—wanting to stay out of sight, I had gone deep into the forest—and now the night's darkness had painted it completely black. I couldn't see anything.
The fading consciousness snapped back alert—and I recognized just how badly things had gone.
"...Oh no. ...Are you serious."
The sweat that had been washed away by rain began to seep out again.
Out here, no streetlamp reached.
I had never calculated for it becoming this dark.
In this blackness—even if I had managed to kill him right here according to plan, I wasn't sure I could have gotten him into the grave without problems.
What should I do?
There was absolutely no way to find the shovel tonight.
If I pushed any deeper into the forest, I might lose all sense of direction and genuinely get lost.
Even if being 'lost' was an exaggeration, twisting an ankle on a root was a real possibility.
Right—there was another camp shovel in the tool shed at home.
Going back for it and giving up on this one for tonight—that was an option.
There was no name engraved on that shovel, of course.
But it was apparently a rare foreign import from some specialty store—so...
I didn't want to leave it here.
But in this darkness, there was nothing I could do.
Give up—go home.
There was a battery lantern at the house. I could search by its light.
But could a lantern really illuminate this absolute darkness well enough?
A single lantern flame in the dead of night.
It would certainly draw attention. And as for whether its faint light would be enough to find the shovel—that was doubtful too.
More to the point—I had been leaving the body lying out there for quite a long time now.
The fear that had been dulled by exhaustion finally began to stir.
Right now—hiding the body had to be the absolute top priority.
A found shovel wouldn't cause much of a stir, but a found body was different. Find it—and it would end up the same as Satoshi's situation.
First—go back to the tool shed at home.
Prepare the lantern.
It was dark enough that I might need a light just to dig the grave.
And get a fresh shovel.
Then—burying the body was first priority.
After that, come back here, find the shovel, and recover it.
Exhausted as I was—I still had all this left to do.
But there was no point in complaining. If I let any of this slip—everything would come to nothing.
I had slumped to the ground without realizing it.
I set my back teeth together hard and forced my reluctant body to stand.
The night was long.
In all the nights I had ever lived through, I had never known one so long.
When would it end? It went on and on.
